speaker: interviewer
text: Toby Fluek. November 16, 1995. We were at your house on the holidays. And do you have any other memories of any Hanukkah or Purim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Hanukkah I remember my father used to light the Hanukkah lamp with oil. He used to pour the oil in-- into compartments and put a piece of thread, cotton thread, and light the Hanukkah. The celebrations were very simple. It wasn't the celebration like we celebrate Hanukkah here, or Purim. Purim was-- in the shtetls, the Purim was celebrated. Children got to dress up and so forth. In the village, I was the only child, so there was no-- there was not much celebration. I remember on Hanukkah my mother gave me a penny, a [INAUDIBLE]. Up to five pennies. I didn't get more than five pennies. That was the Hanukkah gift. Just once a week. That's it. [LAUGHS] That was it. In fact, I started writing a book about Hanukkah, and there wasn't much to [LAUGHS] that. You know, we did the-- we makes the celebrations so festive. And let's say Saturdays, we used to dress-- we used to get new clothing for the major holidays, like for Passover or for Rosh Hashana. We used to-- I remember dressing up. My sister made me a new coat. The new coat was done from a old coat that the American relatives sent to us. So she turned the material inside out, because the coat was faded. And that's how she made me the coat. And I was so proud to wear that navy blue coat. And then we used to get new shoes for the holidays. And we used to go shpatzir, for walks, and to show off the new clothing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close with the Jewish community? Did you gather together? Politics? Talked? Did you ever remember your parents sitting with somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a neighbor, a non-Jewish neighbor. He was-- he was a-- active in the church very much. And he was friendly with us. And he used to come in and talk politics very often. Also, my sister's boyfriend used to come in and speak politics many times. I remember once it was brought up how America will help Poland, you know, with the economy and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the impression of America? I know many people moved there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, America was the golden Medina. Well, we always thought that American relatives are rich, you know? We didn't realize how hard they work and so forth. So-- in fact, if anybody came from America, he wore a nice suit. So that meant riches. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people come back from America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a-- a peasant man came to visit his family. And-- and now, the peasants didn't wear suits. They wore just the shabby-- shabby jackets and then the-- they wore the dungarees, actually. Something-- the pants similar to dungarees. So if a man came dressed in a suit, so he was, you know a rich man. From the Jewish people, I don't-- nobody came back to Poland, to our village.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first feeling in your village of something happening that could be a sign of danger to the whole community? War? That something was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a-- a bit of talk about war, that the war might start. And-- but somehow, I was so naive. I remember myself, like a-- as a little girl, but then I think of it, I was 14 already when the war started. But I never went to a city. I maybe was once to Brody and once to [INAUDIBLE]. And I was so child-- you know, how shall I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Naive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naive, right. So I really didn't know what-- what war meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of course, my parents talked about it. And they were nervous. And I remember the-- when the war started, I woke up in the morning and all the church bells were ringing and a bugle was sounded. And I was asking my mother, what is going on? And she was very upset. And then she told me that a-- a war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, my mother went through the First World War, so she went through a lot. But, of course, we didn't know what to expect, what was coming, you know, with the Second World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard of the sounds of war at what point? What year was it quite apparent?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39. In '39, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still attending school? Starting school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was finishing school in '39, the seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. In '39, I was in the Polish school. And the Russians came-- in '39, the Russians occupied our region. So in-- in '39, '40, I attended another year of school. And when the Russians occupied, we were nervous because we heard stories that they sent people to Siberia and so forth. But they left us alone, because we weren't really-- we weren't rich people. We were, you know, sort of on the poor side. Also, I remember my mother liked to consider herself a middle class. That was a big thing. Anyway, they did send out some rich peasants to Siberia that were very, you know, rich. There was very little change. Of course there was a shortage of food at the time. And otherwise, things didn't change much, and we got used to the way of life. What was the difference that the Communists organized. And they wanted my parents should join the Communist Party. And of course, my mother didn't want to join. And in the school, I was encouraged to join the Komsomolka. That's the Communist youth organization. And my mother also told me to try to avoid to join.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember we were at the recital, and the-- the girls wore red scarves, the one-- the girls that belonged to the Komsomolka. And I, myself, and another few girls, didn't belong to the Komsomolka, so that we were sort of put in a corner. You felt that? It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt, yeah, the difference. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but they didn't force you? It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't force me, no. They didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' political feelings, in general? Do you have any sense of how they felt? Did they see themselves as Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We-- I don't think they did see themselves. We were-- there was no politics really, this-- not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zionism? Did it enter into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, neither did Zionism. We were just farmers. I mean, we didn't know, but-- at least I didn't hear much of Zionism in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish. Newspapers in Yiddish in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, we-- we didn't have newspapers. We didn't get newspapers. And I'm trying to figure out if my father knew how to read Polish. I know my mother knew how to write, and she-- I think she did know how to read. But in the villages, people didn't read newspapers. Like I mentioned this-- this-- he was a Ukrainian. And people were self-educated, for that matter, especially the peasants. And he was very much into politics, so he used to come and discuss politics. He used to get a paper. Unfortunate-- then I had a cousin of mine, in fact, moved in. Like, every few years, we had somebody else in our house living in that one room, the second room. So he used to get a paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you believe it? I don't-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he come from, your cousin? Where did he-- why did he stay with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived-- he was born in Czernica. He got married to Sasov-- a girl from Sasov. And then somehow they moved to our village again. Or was it when he was-- when he got married, I think. He was-- he got married to a girl from Sasov, but when he got married, he lived temporarily in our house for a few years. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your uncle's son from the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's son. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember they had a child. In fact, I used to babysit, watch their little girl. And the little girl got very sick, and she-- she died. She couldn't be helped. I don't know what-- what the sickness was. But it was a very sad episode. That they took her to the doctor [COUGHS] and then they, excuse me, they couldn't do nothing for her. And-- and she was in the room crying. Terrible, yeah. I was very upset. I was quite young at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move on now into 1940. You're finishing school with the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished school with the Russians. And in 19-- and we heard stories of where-- of west-- what's going on in western Poland. We heard that the Germans cut off the beards from-- and they cut the beards of Jewish men. And they mistreated Jews in-- in western Poland. And we sort of was get-- were getting the idea what Hitler is trying to do to the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So many people were talking about leaving their homes to go to Russia. We-- it was very easily to go-- to-- move to Russia. So I remember my sister's boyfriend came and was talking, and my sister was-- wanted to go. And my mother also was-- would consider going. They were talking about it. And my father didn't want to leave the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, they don't think of farmers. They consider only doctors, lawyers. They go after those people, but they wouldn't touch us. And, you know, in Europe, when the father said something, that was the decision. Nobody went against him. So we decided to-- to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about other Jewish families? Did some others leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody from our village left, no. Somebody from [INAUDIBLE] left. In fact, later, you know, they used  to make a joke out of it that she left to Paris, I believe she went. And then Hitler came to Paris. So she went to Switzerland to another-- so they made a joke of it. Whenever-- wherever she goes, Hitler's following her, you know? So it was a sad joke. And 1941, the Germans occupied our region. There was the-- the Russians had a pact with the Germans that the Russians occupied the eastern Poland and the Germans, western Poland. And then I believe Hitler broke the agreement with them. So I remember those-- they came with those three-wheeler bicycles they call them or whatever. Not bicycles--

speaker: interviewer
text: Motorcycles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Motorcycles, that's right. They came in with their shining boots. And we were all scared. Then I remember soldiers stopped the-- was stationed in the village. And a German soldier stopped talked to us, because we spoke Yiddish, so we could communicate with them. And a young boy, and he was telling us we shouldn't be afraid, that they wouldn't do no harm to us and everything. Very friendly German.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Wehrmacht at this point? It was the army, not the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the army. That was the army, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And soon enough, a few weeks later, they-- the Germans came to-- to look for the gold and silver. And later on, they came to-- to take the men to work. In fact, at the time, they took my-- my sister's boyfriend. They took him to a working camp. And at the time, they killed my uncle Mordche and-- and Matl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the circumstances? Why did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know why they shot them. We really don't know. But later the neighbors there told us--

speaker: interviewer
text: In their house? They were shot in their house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shot outside their house, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the Germans came to take away the gold and silver, did they come into your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They came to the house, and they opened the drawers and they looked all over the-- they ransacked the house, yes. We were supposed to-- the order was to bring to the Germans, to the government, all the gold and silver. And then they came-- they followed up to come and check if there was anything left.

words.text: they followed up to come and

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of Germans your town? It was a small town.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. No. Two Germans came. Two German soldiers, and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was a stationed group, a troop of Germans around the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they weren't. In fact, very seldom did we see Germans in town. They just came for-- for a reason, if they had to. The-- then another time they-- like, we were suppose-- we were taxed so heavily that if we gave all the wheat away, we would have no wheat for our own use. So my father gave a certain amount of wheat to the Germans, and then he felt-- you know, he will explain them that we need it for our family. So they came to-- to collect, you know, the-- the wheat. And our-- my mother used to be friendly with the mayor of the town. She used to cook-- if the mayor invited some officials, so he used to come to my mother to ask to cook the dinner for them. So she was friendly with him, so when-- during war, they used to tip us off if the Germans were coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they tipped us off at the time that the Germans were coming to see that we didn't pay-- give the wheat to the Germans, my father and mother and brother ran away to the woods to hide. And we-- the girls, the three girls-- were left on the farm. And my father told us, you have to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what we were doing, we were working in the barn shredding the wheat so when they come, they should see that we are working and so forth. And my father explained it to us what we should say, that we need the wheat for ourselves and we gave as much as we could. Of course, they arrested us. They took us in to jail. And we were in Brody jail for a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the town? In Czernica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Czernica. In Brody. The jail was in Brody. There was no jail in Czernica.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were three weeks. My-- I had a aunt in Brody, too. She sued to bring us packages of food there. Excuse me, the aunt in Brody, whose sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my father's sister in Brody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Father's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was Matl. My aunt in Czernica was a different name. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Rifka? Did you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifka, right. Czernica was Rifka, right. Anyway, my mother went to the officials from the jail, and she bribed them. And they let us out. In fact, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a farmer, his wife, they also paid-- had to pay taxes. Of course, not as much as Jews. And his wife was also arrested, I believe, for not paying-- giving the taxes, not paying the taxes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this farmer came to pick us all up, and we-- with a horse and buggy. So we were-- I remember going home to Czernica with a horse and buggy and this farmer and his wife that was in jail also with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told us, she said she appreciate very much, because my mother helped him to get out his wife. So she used to-- she said at the time, anything I could help you at anytime, please call on me. So when I ran away from the ghetto, I came to her, and she gave me the start.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me ask you, were the jails filled with people at this point in Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no contact. I had no way of knowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in jail with your other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we had a small cell, a dingy cell, with my two sisters. That's all. I-- I didn't see anybody else, so I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran-- I'm sorry. Who ran the jail? The Germans or the Polish? Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish. The Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat you well in the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: They fed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They fed us, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't hurt you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't hurt us, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So it wasn't traumatic for you? You were scared, but not--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were scared, yeah. But is wasn't-- the-- one of the guards-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call it? Asked my sister-- he wanted to, you know, to have sex with her. But she refused, and he left her alone. He left her. He said something that he would let her go home if she would have sex with him, something to that-- I remember my sister was-- came in and-- and she told us. Like, he take-- took her out from the cell, and-- and she said she refused and he left her alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister. He left her alone. He didn't bother us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there three weeks. After we were freed, I think the guard told my mother if we were left another week, we would have-- they would send us to concentration camp. That's the only thing I heard of concentration camp, but of course, we had no idea what concentration camp was all about. So now you went back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back home. We stayed at home, I don't know, another few months or something. And that was 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: The end? The beginning? Cold? What was it-- was it cold when you were in the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was toward the fall, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fall of '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it seems such a short while, because in the fall, we-- it could have been October, something like that, November. Because I believe in November we went to the ghetto. The order came that all the Jews should leave their homes and go to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were also, in a way, treated humanely from-- by the mayor of the town, that the mayor supplied a horse and buggy. In other words, a farmer came with a horse and buggy so we could load up everything we wanted. Of course, we loaded all with food. We took potatoes and wheat and flour, onions, whatever we could. And wood, we took, you know, to-- to be able to heat the rooms. So we left our house. I remember my-- my father-- we gave away to the peasants all our belongings-- the sewing machine, the furniture, and so forth. And my father wrote out some document. By the way, he did write. [LAUGHS] He wrote that document. That came to mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wrote some kind of a document or agreement that he's selling the house to the neighbor, otherwise he felt the neighbor should have the house instead else should take it. And with the idea that when we come home, we should get back the house. We had no idea what-- what was waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went with the horse and buggy. We brought all the stuff to the ghetto. We were placed in one room with another family, or maybe two. I don't remember. It was quite crowded in the ghetto. Excuse me. Your brother was with you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my two sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we came to the ghetto, when you walked out, you could see people laying on the sidewalk, laying in the hallways, dying from starvation. And people were begging. And I used to come in the house and tell my mother that we-- we should help them. And my mother said, how can we help such a crowd? I mean, my mother was helping my-- Lonya. Lonya's sister was in the ghetto, and her father. Who is Lonya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lonya was my sister's boyfriend. So Lonya was in the camp. Now, he was dead at the time. And we-- Lonya was in the camp, and also my mother-- my mother was always, like we said, my mother could get anywhere. She always was a fighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she traveled to Lack was the name of the town. How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lack, L-A-C-K. Lack, a working camp. So she went to that town, and she tried to talk to some people who got contacts with the leaders from the camp. And it was understood that he will be freed. Again, she paid some money to these people, and it was understood that-- she, like, bribing them-- that he will be freed. And a day before, he was hit over his-- over the head with a metal-- with a metal something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bar or something. And he was killed. So he died, and then my sister really was falling apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Lonya's sister and father went to the ghetto. So my mother used to bring them food very often. And they were very distraught, you know. So-- and she used to help. There was-- and Matl. She used to help them sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we came. We had enough food, so didn't starve. And other people already were starving, because in the city, people would make a living from each day they, you know, they-- they used to hunt, and that's how they make their money. And in the ghetto, they used to give out-- there was a Judenrat in the ghetto, and they used to give rations, bread of whatever. But of course, it was-- they were very small rations. People couldn't survive. And people died daily, you know. I remember that sight-- I never forget-- two people, two men, would pull a wagon with the corpses on the wagon. They used to collect the-- in other words, the Germans ordered the Jews to pick up the corpses. Instead of have a horse and buggy pull the wagon, they made the Jews pull the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, describe what-- the part of town that was the ghetto. Did you have walls around the ghetto? Fences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was only a wire fence. May i take a drink of water? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There were wire fences. And many people used to sneak out of the ghetto. And to-- the peasants also used to come to the wired fences and-- to sell some potatoes and bread. And they were asking outrageous prices, so very few people had the money to-- to buy. And then, there were guards around. So if a guard came around to see the transactions, he would take away the-- the-- the food and beat up the-- the Jewish person. And the peasants used to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they buildings of apartments? What did it look like where you were staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in a large-- there was-- it was about a three story-- three-story building. It was facing the marketplace. And next door to that building was a brewer-- a brewery. Now, most houses in the ghetto were small houses. And there was a hospital in the ghetto. In fact, my sister got typhoid, and she wound-- she was in that hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lajcie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lajcie.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where she perished. In fact, they burned the hospital with all the people in it. In fact, I-- I painted a painting of that. It was a very difficult painting to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were now -- this was at what point in the ghetto that your sister was taken to hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1942, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year? Do you remember what you were wearing when you went to see her or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- when our-- let me go back. I-- there was a panic in the ghetto that the liquidation would start. So my sister and myself ran away from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah and yourself? Sarah and myself ran away from the ghetto. My sister wanted the whole family to run away, but again, my father was the type that he would be there. He wanted to stay there. He didn't want to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's leave it now. We'll go on to the next tape and discuss how you got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think I skipped a lot. I have to get back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
